category_title: Adolescents with ASD exhibiting attention-seeking out-of-seat and assaultive behaviors
student_description: Brian is a 12-year-old with ASD whose morning lesson difficulties include leaving his table without permission and, at times, physical aggression toward others. His behavior is attention-seeking and often occurs when academic tasks become difficult, especially when he walks toward the door and checks whether adults are watching. The evidence shows that he needs attention to be redirected away from escape/attention bids in the moment, while long-term success depends on highly personalized, collaborative, self-managed contracts rather than standard school rewards.

criteria.rationale: Brian’s leaving the table is attention-seeking, so immediate adult attention to the behavior can strengthen the very pattern the tutor wants to reduce.
criteria.check: The tutor’s response withholds unnecessary attention, lecturing, pleading, or repeated prompts for Brian’s out-of-seat behavior when it is safe to do so.

criteria.criterion_name: Attention shifted to seated appropriate behavior
criteria.rationale: Brian needs attention to become more available for appropriate table behavior than for leaving the table.
criteria.check: The tutor visibly gives attention, praise, or reinforcement to students who are appropriately seated or engaged rather than making Brian’s leaving the center of attention.

criteria.rationale: Because Brian may move from out-of-seat behavior to physical aggression, planned ignoring must not allow unsafe peer contact to continue.
criteria.check: The tutor maintains a low-attention response while also preventing or stopping unsafe behavior toward others in a calm, non-rewarding way.
